title: Clean Architecture
author: Robert C. Martin
subtitle: A Craftsman's Guide to Software Structure and Design
description: A comprehensive guide to software architecture that distills decades of experience into principles for building maintainable, testable, and flexible systems. Martin presents the rules of good architecture as universal, independent of technology choices — whether you are building a monolith, microservices, or serverless. The book introduces the SOLID principles, component design principles, and the infamous 'Clean Architecture' pattern that combines hexagonal architecture, DCI, and BCE.
keyIdeas: Architecture should be independent of frameworks, databases, and UI
The Dependency Rule: source code dependencies must point inward, toward abstractions
Use cases are the central organizing principle of architecture
Boundaries separate core business logic from external concerns
SOLID principles are the foundation of clean architecture
Screaming architecture: the structure should announce what the system does
Components should be independently deployable and testable
Decoupling at the architectural level enables late decisions
Frameworks are tools, not the structure of your application
A good architecture maximizes the number of decisions not made
keyTakeaways: Architecture is about intent, not frameworks — the structure should reveal what the system does
The Dependency Rule is the central law: source code dependencies must point only inward toward abstractions
Use cases should be the center of your architecture, not the database or the web framework
Decouple at architectural boundaries — use interfaces to create plugin architectures
Frameworks are tools to be used, not structures to be conformed to
Good architecture leaves options open — it defers decisions about databases, web servers, and frameworks
SOLID principles apply at the class level; component principles scale the same thinking
Testing should not require a database, web server, or any infrastructure
Architecture is about cost — good architecture minimizes the lifetime cost of a system
Every architectural boundary should support the option to split into microservices later
whoShouldRead: Software architects and senior engineers designing complex systems
Developers who want to understand the 'why' behind design patterns and principles
Technical leaders responsible for system longevity and maintainability
Any programmer who has ever been paralyzed by framework coupling
